attendees.note: Declined invite — sent apologies via email

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay, we're missing Priya and Kevin and Jordan, but I think the main work today is technical so let's go ahead. Tom, Aisha — good to have you both. Tom, do you want to walk us through what you've got so far?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah, absolutely. So I've been working on the preliminary data mapping and I'm going to share my screen. I also wanted to use this call to float a question about the migration approach, because something came up when I was designing the architecture.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Sure, go ahead.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: So we scoped this as a phased migration — Phase 1 read-only, Phase 2 write. But when I started modeling the FHIR resource graph, I realized the read and write surfaces share about 80% of the same transformation logic. If we build Phase 1, we're basically building most of Phase 2 anyway, and then we have to maintain two separate deployment configurations, two validation suites, two UAT cycles. From an engineering standpoint, I think we can do this in a single cutover and actually reduce risk. The key is a really solid integration testing harness before we go live. Aisha, do you want to speak to that?

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Yeah, so I've been building out our test framework, and I think if we have the mapping approved by the 22nd we can have a full integration test suite covering both read and write endpoints by the end of January. That gives us two weeks of UAT before the February 14th cutover. I'd actually feel more confident in a single-phase with thorough testing than two phases where you're validating a partial system.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's interesting. My concern with the phased approach was always that 30-day stabilization window — that's a lot of overhead on our operations team running two systems in parallel.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Diana made the call on phased for risk management reasons, but... if the technical argument is sound I can bring this back to her. Tom, can you put together a quick one-pager on the risk comparison? Single-phase vs. two-phase, pros and cons?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Absolutely, I'll have that to you tomorrow.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. I think we can decide this now actually — Marcus, you're comfortable with single phase if DataBridge has the test coverage?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Yeah, if Aisha's confident in the testing I'm fine with it. Saves us the parallel operation headache.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Let's call it decided then. Single-phase cutover. Tom, still plan to write the risk comparison doc, but we'll go with single-phase unless Diana overrules. I'll send her a note after this call.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Perfect. I'll update the architecture accordingly. This actually simplifies our deployment model quite a bit.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Noted. Let me pull up the action items from kickoff — Tom, on the data mapping draft for the 15th, are we still on track?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes, you'll have it tomorrow. Aisha has been helping prep the initial field inventory so it's in decent shape.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Tom, I wanted to ask — Marcus sent over the custom lab fields document last week. Were those factored in?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes, I got those. There are a few that I think we'll handle as FHIR extensions. I'll flag them clearly in the mapping document so Marcus and Linda can review. Some of them are going to require a judgment call from your side about what the expected behavior should be when those fields are null.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Linda, can you take the lead on reviewing those specifically? You know the lab system better than I do.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Sure, I'll review those sections and send my comments directly to Tom.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Great. Okay, let me share my screen and walk through what we have so far. Give me a second to pull it up.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Take your time.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Alright, you should be able to see the mapping spreadsheet. I'm going to start with the patient demographics section — that's Tab 1. Each row is a source field from the HL7 v2 feed, and the columns map across to the FHIR Patient resource. The third column is the FHIR path, the fourth is the transformation rule, and the fifth is the criticality tier.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Can you zoom in a bit on the PID segment?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure. So PID-3 is the patient identifier — we're mapping that to Patient.identifier. We pull the first repetition where the identifier type code is MR, which is the medical record number. Any other repetitions we're treating as secondary identifiers and mapping to additional Patient.identifier entries.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: That's the right approach. I flagged in my prior notes that PID-3 sometimes has encounter IDs in the wrong repetition — do you have handling for that?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes — Aisha built a validation step that checks for the identifier type code before we map. If it's not an MR-typed identifier it doesn't go into Patient.identifier.value as the primary. We log any mismatched types to a validation report.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Good. That was exactly the issue we had in 2022.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Moving to PID-5 — patient name. We're mapping to Patient.name. This is standard HumanName — family, given, prefix. We handle the case where the suffix field PID-5.5 is populated. One thing I flagged — about 8% of your records have a blank PID-5.2 for the given name. FHIR treats given name as optional but some downstream consumers may not. We're mapping it as-is and logging the blanks.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That 8% — is that a known data quality issue in our source system?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Looks like it. It's consistent across the sample we got. Not random noise.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Okay. I'll loop in our data quality team on that separately. It's not something DataBridge needs to fix — it's a source issue.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Agreed. We just need a documented policy on how to handle it at the integration layer. For now we're planning to pass through as-is. If you want us to reject records with missing given names, we can do that, but my recommendation is to log and pass through.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Log and pass through is fine. We'll fix the source data quality issue separately.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good. Moving on — PID-7 is date of birth, mapping to Patient.birthDate. Standard ISO 8601 format. No issues there. PID-8 is administrative sex — we're mapping to Patient.gender using the FHIR value set. Your source uses M/F/U — we map those to male/female/unknown. The 'other' value in FHIR doesn't have a direct equivalent in your source.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: We've actually had requests from clinical staff to add a separate gender identity field. Is that something we can incorporate?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: FHIR R4 has the individual-genderIdentity extension. We can include it but it would need to be populated in the source system for us to map it. Does your HL7 feed carry gender identity anywhere?

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Not yet. It's on the roadmap for the source system but not live.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Then I'd recommend we stub the extension in the mapping document for future use but don't implement it in this build. We can add it as a change request when your source system is ready.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's a reasonable approach. Document it as future scope.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: I'll add a 'future scope' column to the mapping doc for items like that. Good. Let me jump to the encounter section — that's where things get more interesting. The MSH and EVN segments plus PV1 all feed into the FHIR Encounter resource.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: PV1-2 is the patient class — inpatient, outpatient, emergency. We map that to Encounter.class using the ActEncounterCode value set. PV1-3 is the assigned patient location — we're mapping that to Encounter.location. The location codes in your source system don't match the FHIR location resource IDs, so we need a crosswalk table.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: The location crosswalk — that's something we'd need to provide you, right? We'd know our internal location codes.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Exactly. I've flagged that in the mapping doc as an Acme-to-provide item. It's not blocking the build but we'll need it before UAT.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll put together the location code crosswalk. Linda, can you help with the clinical unit codes?

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Yes. I'll pull that from the master facility table.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus and Linda provide location code crosswalk to Tom by January 20th.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That works. One more thing on encounters — PV1-44 and PV1-45 are admit and discharge date-times. We map those to Encounter.period. The tricky ones are encounters that don't have a discharge date yet — active encounters. FHIR handles that as an open period, so we leave Encounter.period.end null. That's valid but some consuming applications may handle it differently.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: We have about 12% of encounters that are open on any given day. That's normal for our inpatient census.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good to know. We'll make sure that's tested explicitly. Aisha will add open-encounter scenarios to the test suite.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: One thing I want to flag for the record — the architecture we're now going to single-phase — this affects the infrastructure footprint. CloudPeak will need to provision for the full read/write stack from day one rather than standing up the read environment first. The security group configuration is also going to be different. I'll need to make sure Priya has the updated architecture doc.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Right, I'll loop in Priya after the call. Actually, can you send the updated architecture doc directly to Priya when it's ready, Tom?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Will do.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll send Priya a note to expect it. I want to make sure she knows the phasing decision changed.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah definitely loop her in, there are implications for her environment setup.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay, let's keep going through the mapping. Tom, what's next?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Diagnostic results — that's the most complex section and where the custom lab fields come in. The OBR and OBX segments map to the FHIR DiagnosticReport and Observation resources. Let me flip to Tab 3.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: OBX-2 is the value type — numeric, string, coded entry. Each value type maps to a different Observation.value element. Numeric goes to valueQuantity with the unit from OBX-6. Coded entries go to valueCodeableConcept. We handle all the standard LOINC-coded observations without issue.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: What about the local lab codes? We have about forty observations that don't have LOINC codes — they use our internal coding system.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Right, those are the custom ones from the document Marcus sent. For those, we're proposing to include both the local code and, where we could find a candidate LOINC code, a secondary coding. Some of the fifteen custom fields in Marcus's doc have close LOINC equivalents. About six of them don't — those we'll map with only the local code system identifier set to your OID.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Can you show me which six you're proposing to carry as local-only?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure, Tab 4 — the custom extensions tab. The ones in red are local-only. You've got the lactate dehydrogenase isoenzyme panel, the two proprietary cardiac biomarker fields, the regional immunosuppression protocol level, and two of the point-of-care device readouts.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: The cardiac biomarker fields — those are from our vendor-specific panel. I'll check with the lab whether there's an industry code we should be using. Give me until the 20th on those.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: No problem. I'll leave those flagged in the doc. If you find a LOINC code we'll add it. If not, local-only is a valid FHIR implementation.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Tom, how are you handling the OBX-8 abnormal flags? We have a custom coding scheme — LL, L, N, H, HH, and a few proprietary ones.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good catch. OBX-8 maps to Observation.interpretation using the HL7 ObservationInterpretation value set. The standard LL/L/N/H/HH codes map directly. The proprietary ones — do you have documentation on what they mean?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I have a code table somewhere. I'll dig it up.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That would help. For any proprietary code we can't map to the standard value set, we'll include it as a local coding alongside the mapped code. But we'll need to know the semantics.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus sends Tom the abnormal flag code table with definitions.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Moving on — OBR maps to DiagnosticReport. OBR-4 is the universal service identifier — maps to DiagnosticReport.code. OBR-14 is the specimen received datetime, OBR-22 is the report status datetime. These map to DiagnosticReport.issued. We track the full specimen-to-report timeline in the FHIR resource.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: What about the ordering provider? We need to make sure the result is linked back to the ordering physician.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: OBR-16 is the ordering provider — maps to DiagnosticReport.resultsInterpreter when it's the reading physician, and to the associated ServiceRequest.requester for the ordering physician. We also maintain the link between DiagnosticReport and the originating Encounter.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Good. The encounter linkage is important — our downstream analytics depend on it.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: It's implemented. Every DiagnosticReport.encounter reference will be populated if the associated encounter exists in the system.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Tom, let me ask a broader question while we're on the lab section. What's Aisha's approach to test coverage for diagnostic results? That's a high-stakes data class.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: So Aisha's plan is to build test cases from a synthetic dataset that covers: each observation value type — numeric, string, coded; each abnormal flag variant including the proprietary ones once we have the code table; result statuses including preliminary, final, corrected, and cancelled; and edge cases like missing OBX-8 and null result values. She's targeting around 200 discrete test scenarios for the lab section alone.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's comprehensive. We'll want to see the test case list before UAT so we can make sure our own UAT scenarios don't duplicate too much.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: I'll have Aisha share her test scenario list. She was planning to send it alongside the mapping doc anyway.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Let's continue. Tom, what's after diagnostics?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Medication orders and administration — that's Tab 5. RXO and RXA segments map to MedicationRequest and MedicationAdministration respectively. This is a Tier 1 criticality section — hard reject on any mapping failure. The stakes are too high.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Agreed. I'd add allergy records to that same tier.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes, allergies are in Tab 6 — AL1 segment maps to AllergyIntolerance resource. That's also Tier 1. Any allergy record that fails validation gets hard rejected with an error code sent back to the source system.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: What's the error handling flow when a hard reject happens? Does it go to a dead letter queue?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. Failed messages go to a dead letter queue in Amazon SQS. We'll have a monitoring dashboard — we'll send you the access details — and you can configure alerts for any dead letter queue activity. Tier 1 failures generate an immediate alert.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Who on the Acme side monitors that queue?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That would be our integration team — I'll designate someone. But I want to make sure we have runbook documentation for how to investigate and reprocess a failed message.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's part of the operational runbook we deliver before cutover. We'll walk your team through it during the handoff.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Tom, let's keep moving — we have about twenty minutes left and I want to make sure we get through the architecture discussion.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Let me flip to the architecture diagram. One second. Okay — you can see the updated stack. The key change from the phased plan is that we're now provisioning the full read/write FHIR API surface from day one. This means the CloudPeak environment needs to support the complete data model from the start, rather than the read-only subset.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: What does that mean for the compute sizing?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: It's modestly larger — maybe 20% more compute headroom in the transformation layer because we're handling both inbound reads and outbound writes in the same service. The good news is Fargate auto-scaling handles burst capacity, so the base provisioned tier doesn't need to be massive.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll relay that to Priya so she and Jordan can adjust the capacity plan. Tom, can you include a compute requirement section in the architecture doc?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Absolutely. I'll include recommended task sizes for Fargate, target concurrency, and expected resource utilization at peak load.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: What about the API authentication layer? Our downstream consumers — the network partners — how do they authenticate to the FHIR API?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: We're implementing SMART on FHIR for external client authorization. That's the standard for FHIR API access. Each downstream partner gets a client ID and secret, and they use an OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow to obtain a bearer token. The token is scoped to specific FHIR resource types based on the partner's access rights.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And the Acme-to-DataBridge path — the inbound HL7 feed — how is that secured?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's a private network path — MLLP over a site-to-site VPN. No public internet exposure. The VPN terminates at CloudPeak's VPC and the MLLP listener is in a private subnet with security group rules that only allow traffic from Acme's IP ranges.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. That aligns with what Dev Rajan and Jordan need to coordinate on.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Exactly. I'll make sure the architecture doc includes the specific security group rules DataBridge needs CloudPeak to configure.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Tom, one concern I have — what happens if the HL7 feed goes down? If Rhapsody restarts, do we lose messages?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: No. The Amazon MQ broker persists messages to disk before the transformation service picks them up. If the feed drops, messages accumulate in the queue and are processed in order when the feed comes back. We don't lose anything.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: And if the transformation service goes down?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Same answer — messages sit in the queue. Fargate will restart the task within about 30 seconds. Queue depth will build up but nothing is lost. We'll include queue depth monitoring in the dashboard so your team can see if there's a backlog building.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's good. Our biggest concern with the old HL7 system was message loss during restarts.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: The persistent queue is exactly designed to address that. It's one of the main reliability improvements in the new stack.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Let me ask about retry logic. If a message transformation fails — not a hard reject, but a transient error — does it retry automatically?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. We have exponential backoff retry on transient errors — network timeouts, downstream service unavailability. Three retries with increasing backoff. If it fails all three retries, it moves to the dead letter queue with full error context so your team can investigate.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Can messages be manually reprocessed from the dead letter queue?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. The runbook will include the procedure for moving a message back to the main queue after the underlying issue is fixed. It's a few clicks in the AWS console.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Alright, I think we've covered the main technical items. Let me check the time — we have about twelve minutes left. Tom, is there anything in the mapping doc that you're still uncertain about and want Acme input on before you finalize v1?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Two things. One — the MRG segment for patient merges. We're mapping that to a linked Patient record pattern in FHIR, but the exact behavior depends on how Acme wants patient identity managed post-merge. That needs a conversation with Marcus's team.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Patient merges are infrequent but critical. I'll connect you with our MPI team — they have a specific workflow for that.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Perfect. Second item — the document section headers in the CDA attachments. We have some OBX records with CDA documents embedded as base64. We're mapping those to FHIR DocumentReference. The question is whether the downstream network partners need access to those documents or just the discrete data elements.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's a question for the network partnership team — that's outside my scope. Diana would know.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll ask Diana. For now, Tom, include DocumentReference in the mapping and we'll decide whether to expose it in the API.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Done. I'll include it and flag it as pending business decision in the mapping doc.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Marcus, Linda — any other questions for Tom before we close?

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: I think I have enough to do a thorough review when the doc comes out tomorrow. I'll read through the custom extensions tab carefully and send comments directly.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Same. I'll focus on the ADT segments and encounter data. Linda takes the clinical stuff.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That division makes sense. If you can tag your comments in the spreadsheet so I know who raised each one, that'll make the resolution process faster.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Tom, any other dependencies or blockers you want to flag before we wrap?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: The signed BAA — Kevin said he'd have that by January 13th. We're not blocked yet but we need it before we can access any Acme staging data.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Kevin's not on this call but I'll follow up with him on the BAA today. That date is tomorrow so it should already be in flight.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: And the location crosswalk and abnormal flag code table — those are the other open items I need from Acme.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll get both of those to you by the 20th.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's fine, we don't need them for the mapping v1 delivery — just before we finalize the build.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Let me also note — Aisha, you've been mostly listening. Anything you want to add before we close the technical portion?

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Just one thing — for the integration test environment, I'll need access to the HAPI FHIR test server that CloudPeak is standing up. Can someone make sure Jordan has my email so he can send me the connection details when it's ready?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll connect you and Jordan directly after this call.

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Thanks. That's all from me.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Actually one more — Aisha, I want to confirm with you that the test framework will cover the single-phase architecture, not the two-phase. Make sure you're not building against the old deployment model.

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Already updated. When you mentioned the single-phase approach at the start of the call I made a note. The full read/write surface will be covered from day one in the test suite.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Perfect.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright. Good session. Tom, mapping v1 goes out tomorrow — January 15th. Acme, review window is January 15th through 22nd. Rolling comments welcome. Marcus provides location crosswalk and flag code table by the 20th. Aisha and Jordan to connect on test environment access. Tom sends updated architecture to Priya when ready. I'll follow up on the BAA with Kevin today. Anything I missed?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: The MPI team introduction for patient merges.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Right — Marcus connects Tom with the MPI team. Got it.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: And the abnormal flag code table is on the list too.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yes, that's included. Okay — I think we're good. Thanks everyone. This was a productive session.

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Thanks — good to finally meet everyone.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Same. Looking forward to seeing the mapping doc tomorrow, Tom.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: You'll have it.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Tom, one quick thing before we close — you mentioned the FHIR validator. Which version are you using? We've seen inconsistencies across validator versions on some of the extension profiles.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: We're running the HL7 Java validator, version 6.1.14. I'll pin that in the test suite documentation so there's no ambiguity. If you want to run local validation on your end we can use the same version.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: That would be helpful. I'd like to run some validation checks on our end during the review period.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: I'll include the validator setup instructions in a separate doc and link it from the mapping spreadsheet.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good idea. That way the review is grounded in the same tooling we're building against.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Aisha, quick question — will you be available to answer technical questions during our review period if something in the mapping document isn't clear?

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Yes, absolutely. Best to reach me on Slack or email. I'm usually pretty responsive during business hours.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: And for anything more complex I'm available too. Don't wait until the end of the review window if something is blocking your read-through.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. Okay, I want to do one final check — Marcus, Linda, do you feel like you have enough context to do a meaningful review of the mapping doc when it comes out tomorrow? Any gaps we should address now?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I feel good. Today's walkthrough helped a lot — I understand Tom's methodology now, which makes it easier to assess whether the specific mappings are right.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Same. The custom extensions section is the area I'll focus on. I may have more questions once I see the actual field-level decisions, but I know where to send them.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Fair. And I'll add inline notes to the more complex decisions in the doc so the rationale is clear without you having to reverse-engineer it.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That'll save a lot of back-and-forth. Good thinking.

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: I'll also attach a sample output — a few example FHIR resources generated from synthetic HL7 messages — so you can see what the actual JSON looks like, not just the mapping rules.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That would be very helpful. Seeing the actual output is much easier to evaluate than reading a mapping table.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good call, Aisha. Include one example for each major resource type — Patient, Encounter, DiagnosticReport, Observation.

transcript.speaker: Aisha Patel
transcript.text: Will do. I'll have those ready to go with the mapping doc tomorrow.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Perfect. That's a really useful addition. Okay I think we're genuinely done with the substantive content. Let me do a formal recap for the record.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright, let me recap. We're switching to single-phase cutover on February 14th. Tom sends data mapping v1 tomorrow, January 15th. Acme review by January 22nd. Tom sends updated architecture package to CloudPeak — when does that change, Tom?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Same date, January 29th. Once we have the mapping approved we can finalize architecture pretty quickly.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. Same critical path, new migration approach. Anything else?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Just want to make sure Kevin is aware of the phasing change. If there are contract implications...

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good point. Kevin wasn't on this call — I'll send him a note. Tom, you talk to Kevin too?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah I'll let him know.
